title: Dog Back Leg Paralysis: Causes and Emergency Care Guide
meta_desc: If your dog suddenly cannot stand or has paralyzed back legs, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about IVDD and the next steps for recovery.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden hind limb paralysis in dogs is a critical medical emergency requiring immediate veterinary attention to prevent permanent nerve damage. If your pet cannot support their weight or is dragging their legs, transport them to a clinic immediately and capture a video of their movement to assist the triage team.

What causes sudden hind limb paralysis in my dog?

Sudden loss of function in the back legs, often referred to as paraparesis or paraplegia, occurs when the communication between the brain and the hind limbs is interrupted. This is most frequently caused by issues within the spinal cord. Common conditions include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), spinal trauma, or a Fibrocartilaginous Embolism (FCE), which is essentially a stroke in the spine.

Is sudden loss of function in my dog's back legs a medical emergency?

  • Yes, this is a high-urgency medical emergency.
  • Seek veterinary care immediately if your dog is unable to support their weight or is dragging their back legs.
  • Monitor for signs of extreme pain, including shivering, panting, or vocalizing.
  • Do not delay treatment, as it can lead to permanent nerve damage or the loss of deep pain perception, significantly lowering recovery chances.

How can a photo or video help the vet triage my dog's paralysis?

  • Capture a short video of your dog's movement or attempt to move while preparing for transport.
  • Video helps the doctor identify specific issues like "knuckling" (walking on the tops of the paws) or total loss of motor function.
  • Visual data allows the triage team to prioritize your pet's case the moment you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Acute, painful paraparesis and inability to flex or extend the hock can result from arterial thromboembolism, commonly at the distal aortic trifurcation, causing ischemia of pelvic limb muscles and nerves; underlying disorders may include hyperadrenocorticism, hypothyroidism, renal disease, cancer, and heart disease. Clinical signs include loss of flexor and potentially patellar reflexes, decreased sensation distal to the hock, firm and painful gastrocnemius and cranial tibial muscles, possibly cyanotic nails, and weak or absent femoral pulses. Tibial paralysis, damage to the caudal branch of the ischiatic nerve, results in paralysis of the extensors of the hock and flexors of the digits, causing an overflexed hock (dropped hock syndrome) and partially flexed fetlock; the gastrocnemius may appear longer than normal. Acute idiopathic polyradiculoneuritis can manifest as a short-strided gait in pelvic limbs progressing to flaccid tetraparesis or tetraplegia with possible facial and laryngeal weakness; spinal cord reflexes are weak to absent with rapid muscle atrophy, pain perception remains intact, and CSF analysis reveals increased protein with normal cell count.

Chapter: Cardiology, Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1245)

faqs.question: Can a dog recover from back leg paralysis?
faqs.answer: Yes, many dogs regain the ability to walk. Recovery depends on the underlying cause, the severity of the nerve damage, and how quickly medical or surgical intervention is provided.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of sudden paralysis?
faqs.answer: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD) is the most common cause, especially in breeds with long backs like Dachshunds, Corgis, and French Bulldogs.

faqs.question: How should I move my dog if they can't walk?
faqs.answer: Keep the spine as neutral as possible. Use a flat board or a thick, supportive blanket as a stretcher to prevent further spinal shifting during transport.
